medications.drug_name: analgesia (e.g. paracetamol or facility-available options) as sole management
medications.indication_rationale: Pain relief alone does not address the time-critical arterial occlusion and must not delay emergency escalation; the receiving hospital team will manage analgesia appropriately alongside emergency revascularisation.
contraindications_in_this_case: Time-critical limb-threatening ischaemia requiring emergency intervention, not symptomatic management alone

medications.drug_name: topical heat or ice packs, or compression stockings
medications.indication_rationale: Heat risks burns on ischaemic, insensate skin; ice can worsen vasoconstriction; compression stockings would further restrict already-compromised blood flow.
contraindications_in_this_case: Ischaemic, anaesthetic limb at risk of thermal injury or worsened perfusion

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Correct the leg position immediately to dependent (hanging down / lower than the heart, not elevated) and avoid any heat, ice, or compression while awaiting the ambulance.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Elevation reduces perfusion pressure to an already ischaemic limb; dependent positioning uses gravity to help maintain what residual flow remains.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Facility staff should monitor closely for any further deterioration (worsening pain, spreading discolouration, or new systemic symptoms) and report immediately to paramedics on arrival.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Ensures the receiving team has an accurate picture of progression.

patient_education_points.point: The sudden pain, numbness, coldness, and paleness in her leg — especially with her history of poor circulation — suggest a blood vessel supplying her leg has become completely blocked. This needs emergency treatment right away to try to save the leg.
Please call 000 for an ambulance immediately — this needs a hospital with vascular surgery capability, and time really matters here.
Please lower the leg so it's hanging down rather than elevated — elevating it, even though it seems comforting, reduces the little blood flow that may be getting through.
Do not apply any heat packs, ice, or compression stockings to the leg.
Please let the facility physician/on-call GP know about this transfer so her full medical history reaches the hospital team.

follow_up_plan.interval: Superseded by emergency escalation — follow-up arranged by the vascular surgery team and nursing home physician after treatment
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Post-discharge: limb viability and function, wound care if amputation required, secondary prevention review (antiplatelet/statin optimisation), rehabilitation planning with facility care team
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any further deterioration in colour, pain, or new systemic symptoms while awaiting the ambulance — report immediately to paramedics on arrival.

must_include_items: Call 000 for an ambulance immediately, specifying need for vascular surgery capability
Explicit correction to keep the leg dependent, not elevated
Explicit statement that this may be a blocked artery/an emergency
Instruction to avoid heat, ice, and compression
acceptable_alternatives: None — given the narrow limb-salvage window, ambulance dispatch is the only acceptable first action
errors_of_omission: No correction of the elevated leg position
No explicit statement this may be a blocked artery
errors_of_commission: Allowing or endorsing the leg to remain elevated
Recommending analgesia alone as the primary management
Recommending heat packs or compression stockings
Deferring to a routine morning GP/physician review instead of immediate ambulance dispatch
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: CRITICAL CASE: Any response that does not include (1) immediate 000 call specifying vascular surgery need AND (2) explicit correction to dependent limb positioning is an automatic FAIL. This is a T5 case with an approximately 4-6 hour limb-salvage window.
